title: Clean Architecture
objectives: Understand Clean Architecture via a real-life analogy
Know the four concentric layers and the Dependency Rule
Distinguish Entities / Use Cases / Interface Adapters / Frameworks
See when Clean Architecture is worth the ceremony (and when it isn't)

sections.markdown:
**Think of Clean Architecture as an onion — or a well-designed hospital.**

- **The inner core** is the OPERATING ROOM — sterile, single-purpose, doesn't care what building it's in or which surgeon is on shift today. In code: your **Entities** — pure business objects (`User`, `Order`, `Money`).
- **The next ring** is the SURGICAL PROTOCOLS — 'when to operate, what steps in what order'. In code: your **Use Cases** — application-specific business rules (`RegisterUser`, `CalculateShippingCost`).
- **The next ring** is the HOSPITAL STAFF — receptionists, nurses translating between patients and the operating room. In code: **Interface Adapters** — Controllers, Presenters, Repository implementations. They translate between UI and Use Cases.
- **The outermost ring** is the BUILDING ITSELF — floors, walls, wiring, plumbing. Replaceable. In code: **Frameworks & Drivers** — Flutter widgets, Retrofit, Hive, SQLite, HTTP libraries.

**The one crucial rule (the Dependency Rule):** dependencies only point INWARD. Frameworks depend on Adapters, Adapters depend on Use Cases, Use Cases depend on Entities. NEVER the other way. Entities know nothing of Use Cases. Use Cases know nothing of Flutter, Retrofit, or Hive.

**Consequence:** you could replace Flutter with UIKit, or Retrofit with GraphQL, or Hive with SQLite — WITHOUT touching a single line of Use Case or Entity code. That's the whole point. The core business logic is INSULATED from framework choices.

**Why this matters:** Clean Architecture is the enterprise-grade answer to 'how do we build an app that lasts 10 years and outlives every trend?' It's overkill for a hackathon — and essential for a banking, healthcare, or airline app.

**Clean Architecture** (Robert C. Martin, 2012) is a layered architectural pattern with **four concentric circles**: **Entities** (enterprise business rules) → **Use Cases** (application business rules) → **Interface Adapters** (Controllers, Presenters, Gateways) → **Frameworks & Drivers** (Flutter, HTTP libraries, DBs). The **Dependency Rule** says dependencies must point INWARD only. Combined with **dependency inversion** at layer boundaries, this insulates business logic from framework choices — making the app testable, replaceable-at-the-edges, and resistant to churn.
## The four layers

rows: Pure business objects, rules that DON'T change per app: `User`, `Money`, `PolicyRule`
Flutter widgets, Retrofit/Dio, Hive/Isar, HTTP, Firebase

sections.markdown: **The Dependency Rule (the ONE rule):** source-code dependencies must point ONLY inward. Something in the outer ring can know about inner rings; nothing in an inner ring should know about outer rings. Entities don't import Flutter. Use Cases don't import Retrofit. Ever.
## The layers in a Flutter directory structure

sections.code:
lib/
├── domain/                        # Layer 1 + 2: Entities + Use Cases
│   ├── entities/
│   │   ├── user.dart              # class User (pure Dart)
│   │   └── order.dart
│   ├── repositories/              # Interfaces (contracts)
│   │   └── user_repository.dart   # abstract class UserRepository
│   └── usecases/
│       ├── register_user.dart     # class RegisterUser — uses User + UserRepository
│       └── get_orders.dart
│
├── data/                          # Layer 3: Interface Adapters
│   ├── datasources/
│   │   ├── user_remote_datasource.dart   # Retrofit calls
│   │   └── user_local_datasource.dart    # Hive queries
│   ├── models/                    # DTOs (network / DB shapes)
│   │   └── user_dto.dart          # UserDto.toEntity() / User.toDto()
│   └── repositories/
│       └── user_repository_impl.dart     # implements UserRepository
│
└── presentation/                  # Layer 4: Frameworks — Flutter
    ├── screens/
    │   └── login_screen.dart      # widgets
    ├── viewmodels/                # or blocs / notifiers
    │   └── login_viewmodel.dart   # calls RegisterUser use case
    └── widgets/

# Note: the DOMAIN layer has ZERO Flutter imports. It compiles as a
# pure Dart package. That's the litmus test for Clean Architecture.

sections.markdown: ## Sample program — a slice from each layer
## Dependency Inversion — the trick that makes it work

sections.code:
Without dependency inversion (dependencies pointing OUTWARD, wrong):

    Use Case: RegisterUser
         │
         ▼ imports
    Data Layer: UserRepositoryImpl → depends on Retrofit
    
    → RegisterUser now transitively depends on Retrofit.
    → Can't test without HTTP; can't replace Retrofit without touching Use Case.

With dependency inversion (Clean Architecture):

    Use Case: RegisterUser  ────►  UserRepository (interface, in domain layer)
                                            ▲
                                            │  implements
                                            │
    Data Layer: UserRepositoryImpl (in data layer, uses Retrofit)
    
    → Use Case depends only on an ABSTRACT interface it OWNS.
    → Data layer implements what Use Case declared it needs.
    → Swap Retrofit for Chopper: only Data layer changes.
    → Tests inject a FakeUserRepository directly, no HTTP.

The interface is DECLARED in the inner layer (domain) but IMPLEMENTED
by the outer layer (data). That's dependency inversion.

sections.markdown: ## Common mistakes

**Mistake 1: Importing Flutter from domain.** `import 'package:flutter/material.dart';` inside `domain/` breaks the whole architecture. Litmus test: `grep -R 'flutter' lib/domain/` should return NOTHING.
**Mistake 2: Use Cases that call Retrofit directly.** They should call a Repository interface, which the Data layer implements. Otherwise the Use Case depends transitively on Retrofit.
**Mistake 3: DTOs leaking into Use Cases.** The Data layer's `UserDto` (matches JSON) shouldn't reach the Use Case. Convert to a domain `User` at the boundary.
**Mistake 4: One Use Case per API call.** Use Cases model USER ACTIONS (`Register`, `Login`, `PlaceOrder`) — not endpoints. A single Use Case can call several APIs across a workflow.
**Mistake 5: Clean Architecture for a 3-screen todo app.** The ceremony cost isn't justified. Match architecture to app scale.
## Best practices
**1. Keep the litmus test.** No Flutter imports in `domain/`. Enforce with a lint rule if possible.
**2. One Use Case = one user action.** Not one endpoint. Model the business intent, not the URL.
**3. Repository interfaces in `domain`, impls in `data`.** Inversion of control at the layer boundary.
**4. DTOs never leave `data`.** Convert to Entities at the boundary; the Use Case sees only domain objects.
**5. Use DI (get_it, Riverpod) at the composition root** (`main.dart`). That's the ONE place that knows every layer.
**6. Only adopt for apps that will last.** If it's a hackathon or a 2-week MVP, skip it — the layer overhead isn't repaid.
**7. Test each layer in isolation.** Use Cases with fake Repositories; ViewModels with fake Use Cases. No widget pumping needed.
## Practice programs
**Practice 1 (Easy) — Layer directories.** Create `lib/domain`, `lib/data`, `lib/presentation` in a fresh Flutter project. Move a counter into layers.
**Practice 2 (Medium) — Register user flow.** Build the sample: Entity + Use Case + Repository interface + Impl + ViewModel + Screen. Verify domain has no Flutter imports.
**Practice 3 (Medium) — Test the Use Case.** Fake Repository; call `RegisterUser` in a plain Dart test.
**Practice 4 (Hard) — Swap Retrofit for GraphQL.** Only your Data layer should change. Confirm Use Cases and Entities untouched.
**Practice 5 (Expert) — Feature-first + Clean Architecture.** Combine both: `lib/features/user/{domain,data,presentation}/`. Discuss trade-offs vs strict horizontal Clean.
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: What is Clean Architecture and what problem does it solve?**

**A:** Clean Architecture (Robert C. Martin, 2012) is a layered pattern with four concentric circles: **Entities** (business rules), **Use Cases** (application-specific rules), **Interface Adapters** (Controllers/Presenters/Repositories), **Frameworks & Drivers** (Flutter, Retrofit, DBs). The **Dependency Rule** says dependencies point INWARD only — outer layers know inner ones, never the reverse. **Solves:** framework churn (swap Flutter for React Native without touching business rules), testability (business logic testable without any UI/network), long-term maintainability. **Trade-off:** significant ceremony — for apps under ~50k lines it's usually overkill. For enterprise (banking, healthcare, airlines) it's the industry standard.

**Q2: What are the four layers?**

**A:** **(1) Entities (innermost)** — pure business objects that don't change per app: `User`, `Money`, `PolicyRule`. Zero framework dependencies. **(2) Use Cases** — application-specific business rules like `RegisterUser` or `PlaceOrder`. Depend only on Entities and Repository interfaces. **(3) Interface Adapters** — Repository implementations, Controllers/Presenters/ViewModels. Translate between outer frameworks and inner Use Cases. Contain DTOs, JSON mapping, API call wrappers. **(4) Frameworks & Drivers (outermost)** — Flutter widgets, Retrofit/Dio, Hive/Isar, HTTP libraries. Replaceable. Depend inward via interfaces. **Rule:** each layer knows only about layers INSIDE it, never outside.

**Q3: What is the Dependency Rule?**

**A:** Source-code dependencies must point ONLY inward. An outer layer knows about inner layers; inner layers don't know about outer layers. Concretely: **Entities import nothing but Dart core** (no `flutter`, no `dio`, no `hive`). **Use Cases import Entities** — nothing else. **Data layer imports Use Cases + Entities + external libraries** (Retrofit, Hive) — and implements interfaces declared by inner layers. **Presentation imports everything through interfaces**. **Why:** insulates business logic from framework choices. Swap Flutter for something else — inner layers untouched. Swap Retrofit for Chopper — Data layer only. This ONE rule is what makes Clean Architecture worthwhile.

**Q4: What is Dependency Inversion in Clean Architecture?**

**A:** Repository INTERFACES (contracts) live in the INNER layer (domain), IMPLEMENTATIONS live in the OUTER layer (data). So the Use Case depends on an abstract interface it OWNS, and the Data layer PROVIDES an implementation. This inverts the usual arrow: instead of Use Cases depending on Retrofit, Retrofit-code depends on the interface Use Cases declare. **Consequence:** the Use Case doesn't know or care about Retrofit; tests inject a `FakeUserRepository`; production wires the real one at the composition root. This is dependency inversion — the 'D' in SOLID — and it's the mechanism that makes Clean Architecture's layer separation actually possible in code, not just diagrams.

**Q5: When is Clean Architecture worth the ceremony?**

**A:** When the app is EXPECTED TO LAST. Rules of thumb: **(1) 50k+ LOC** — the ceremony pays off. **(2) Team of 5+** — architectural boundaries help onboarding. **(3) Regulated domain (banking, healthcare, aviation)** — the audit story of separated business rules matters. **(4) Multiple front-ends over time** (mobile → web → desktop) — the shared domain layer is huge win. **Not worth it for:** hackathons, MVPs under 2 weeks, single-dev apps, disposable prototypes. Rule: 'YAGNI, but plan for the day you might.' Start feature-first + MVVM; refactor to layered Clean Architecture when the pain justifies it.

**Q6: How does Clean Architecture relate to MVVM?**

**A:** They compose. **MVVM** describes how the PRESENTATION LAYER (outermost) is structured — ViewModel exposes reactive state; View binds to it. **Clean Architecture** describes the WHOLE APP — presentation is just one of four layers. In a Clean + MVVM Flutter app: **View** (widgets) — Layer 4. **ViewModel** — top of Layer 4, calls Use Cases. **Use Case** — Layer 2. **Repository interface** — Layer 2 (in domain). **Repository impl** — Layer 3 (in data). **Entities** — Layer 1 (in domain). So MVVM is 'what the top layer looks like'; Clean Architecture is 'how the whole app is layered'. They're friends, not rivals.

**Q7: What are common mistakes in Clean Architecture implementations?**

**A:** **(1) Domain layer imports Flutter.** The single most common leak. Litmus test: `grep -R 'flutter' lib/domain/` — should return nothing. **(2) Use Cases wrap single API calls.** They should model USER ACTIONS, not endpoints. **(3) DTOs leak from data to domain.** The Repository must convert to Entities at the boundary. **(4) Over-layering** — a 3-screen todo app doesn't need four layers. **(5) One Use Case = one class = one .dart file — every time.** Fine for large apps; bureaucratic for small ones. **(6) Adopting Clean Architecture 'because Uncle Bob said so'.** Adopt it because the app's complexity justifies it. Zealous over-engineering is as bad as under-engineering.

**Q8: How do you test a Clean Architecture Flutter app?**

**A:** Layer by layer, mostly without widgets. **Entity tests** — pure unit tests on business rules (`test('email is valid', () => expect(User(email:'a@b.com',...).hasValidEmail, isTrue));`). **Use Case tests** — inject a fake Repository, verify the Use Case orchestrates correctly (`test('register creates user', () async { … await usecase(); verify(repo.create(_)).called(1); });`). **Repository tests** — integration-level with fake API + fake cache. **ViewModel tests** — inject fake Use Case, verify state transitions. **Widget tests** — just render a screen with a fake ViewModel. **Almost nothing needs `pumpWidget`** except the widget-tree tests. This is Clean Architecture's biggest payoff — a fast, isolated test suite that ships in seconds.

## Real-world usage

- **Enterprise Flutter apps** — banking, insurance, healthcare, airlines almost universally use Clean Architecture.
- **Long-lived apps with multiple platforms** — shared domain layer across Flutter mobile + Flutter Web + backend.
- **Regulated software** — the layer separation gives auditors a clear story of 'where the rules live'.
- **Uncle Bob's own book 'Clean Architecture'** is the canonical text.
- **NOT for hackathons, MVPs, tutorial demos** — the ceremony isn't worth it there.

## Advanced notes

- **Onion Architecture (Jeffrey Palermo)** — near-identical to Clean Architecture; both preceded by Alistair Cockburn's **Hexagonal / Ports & Adapters**.
- **DDD (Domain-Driven Design)** — often combined; Entities + Value Objects + Aggregates map onto Clean Architecture's inner layers.
- **CQRS** — Command Query Responsibility Segregation; complementary pattern.
- **`freezed` + `sealed`** — perfect fit for Clean's immutable data classes.
- **`get_it` / Riverpod** — the two common DI containers in Flutter Clean Architecture.
- **Feature-first Clean** — combine feature-first structure with per-feature Clean layers.
- **Testing strategies** — golden tests for widgets, unit for use cases, integration for repositories.
- **`melos` / monorepos** — split domain / data / presentation into separate Dart packages.
- **Uncle Bob's blog post** — the original 'Clean Architecture' post (2012).

flashcards.back: Entities (business objects) → Use Cases (app rules) → Interface Adapters (Repositories/Controllers) → Frameworks (Flutter/HTTP/DB).
Source-code dependencies point ONLY inward. Outer knows inner; inner never knows outer.
`grep -R 'flutter' lib/domain/` returns nothing. Domain layer has zero Flutter imports.
Repository interfaces in domain; impls in data. Use Case depends on interface it owns.
One user action (Register, Login, PlaceOrder). Not one endpoint.
Enterprise / regulated / long-lived / large team. NOT hackathons or MVPs.

challenges.text: Set up lib/domain, lib/data, lib/presentation directories in a new project.
Litmus test: grep -R 'flutter' lib/domain/. Should be empty.
Implement RegisterUser: Entity + Use Case + Repository interface + Impl + ViewModel.
Unit-test the Use Case using a fake Repository.
Swap the HTTP library from Dio to Chopper; verify Use Case + Entity untouched.
Split domain / data / presentation into 3 Dart packages via melos monorepo.
